semantic_description: A three-dimensional textural element created by increasing multiple stitches from one, working several short rows over just those stitches, then decreasing back to one stitch. The result is a raised bump or ball on the surface of the fabric. At the chart level, a bobble consumes 1 stitch and produces 1 stitch — the expansion and collapse happen within the operation itself. Bobbles are exclusively worked on RS rows; the WS row at that position is just a plain purl.

written_forms: make bobble: (k1, p1, k1, p1, k1) into next stitch [5 sts], turn, p5, turn, k5, turn, p5, turn, k5tog
work a 5-stitch bobble
MB: increase to 5 sts, work 4 rows, decrease back to 1
ambiguity_notes: CYC does not have 'bobble' in the master list but 'MB' (make bobble) is the universal chart abbreviation. The number suffix (MB3, MB5, MB7) indicates how many stitches the bobble is worked from/to. 'B' alone is heavily ambiguous: bead, bobble, or just a size marker — only usable when the legend clearly states 'B = bobble'. Purl Soho uses 'MB' for a 5-stitch bobble. Rowan often defines their own bobble abbreviation per pattern.

Bobble size varies by pattern — 3-stitch, 5-stitch, and 7-stitch bobbles are all common. The abbreviation 'MB' (make bobble) doesn't specify size. Always check the pattern's specific bobble instructions.
A 'popcorn' stitch is similar but the increase stitches are not turned — they are worked, then the first stitch is pulled through the last. Different construction, similar appearance.
'B' and 'MB' are also used for beads in some patterns. Disambiguate via the pattern legend — bobbles typically have a clear bump symbol.

rs: (k1, p1, k1, p1, k1) into the next stitch — 5 stitches from 1
Turn work. Purl 5.
Turn work. Knit 5.
Turn work. Purl 5.
Turn work. Knit 5 together (or sl4, k1, pass 4 over) — back to 1 stitch
Continue working the main row

semantic_description: A textural element similar to a bobble but worked without turning. Increase into one stitch (typically 5 stitches), then pass all the extra stitches over the last one to collapse back to 1. Faster to work than a bobble because there are no short rows. Produces a slightly flatter, rounder bump compared to a bobble.

written_forms: knit 5 into the next stitch, then pass the first 4 stitches over the last stitch one at a time
make popcorn: (k1, p1, k1, p1, k1) into next st, pass 4 over
make popcorn
ambiguity_notes: 'PC' for popcorn is distinct from 'PC5' (5-stitch popcorn). 'Popcorn' and 'bobble' are sometimes used interchangeably in patterns but technically differ: a popcorn is completed on the same row (stitches are not returned to the left needle), while a bobble completes over multiple mini-rows.

rs: (k1, p1, k1, p1, k1) into the next stitch — 5 stitches
Pass the 4th stitch over the 5th (on right needle)
Pass the 3rd stitch over
Pass the 2nd stitch over
Pass the 1st stitch over — back to 1 stitch

semantic_description: A Baltic/Estonian lace technique that creates a small rounded bead on the fabric surface. Unlike a bobble, a nupp spans two rows: on the RS, multiple loops (typically 5-7) are pulled through a single stitch without removing them fully, creating a cluster of loops. On the following WS row, all loops are purled together as one stitch, collapsing the nupp. The result is a tiny, tight bobble. Traditional Estonian lace uses nupps extensively.

written_forms: nupp: (k1, yo, k1, yo, k1) into next stitch on RS; on following WS row, purl all 5 loops together
work nupp: make 5 (or 7) loops from 1 stitch, purl together on return row
ambiguity_notes: Estonian nupp is a tight gathered cluster of (typically) 5 or 7 stitches, knitted together on the return row. 'N' as a stand-alone abbreviation is extremely ambiguous (could be 'no', 'needle', 'nupp', or a size). In Estonian lace patterns by designers like Siiri Reimo or Eevi Maschinen, 'N' or 'N5' is standard. In non-Estonian contexts, write 'nupp' in full or 'N5'/'N7'.
Nupp size varies: 5-loop and 7-loop nupps are most common. A 7-loop nupp is more pronounced but harder to purl together on the WS.
The WS p5tog (or p7tog) is the hardest part — keeping all loops loose on the RS row makes the WS collapse easier.
Open, filled, and triangular chart glyphs can all denote the same nupp technique. In symbol libraries these are often visual variants rather than different knitting operations.

rs: RS row: Into the next stitch, work (k1, yo, k1, yo, k1) — 5 loops from 1 stitch. Keep loops LOOSE.
WS row (next row): Purl all 5 loops together as p5tog — collapses back to 1 stitch

semantic_description: A short-row technique that prevents holes at the turning point. Before turning the work mid-row, the next stitch is wrapped: slip the stitch, bring yarn to other side, return the stitch, then turn. On a later row, the wrap is picked up and worked together with its stitch to hide the gap. Essential for shaping (bust darts, heel turns, shoulder shaping) without bind-offs.

written_forms: wrap and turn: slip next stitch purlwise, bring yarn to other side, return stitch to left needle, turn work
w&t: sl1, move yarn, sl st back, turn
w&t: bring yarn to front, sl next st, bring yarn to back, sl st back, turn work
wrap and turn: slip next stitch, move yarn to opposite side, slip stitch back, turn
ambiguity_notes: Duplicate of w_and_t (structural) — this appears in texture.json. Same abbreviations apply. The texture category placement may reflect use as a shaping tool within a stitch-pattern row rather than a structural row-end turn.
'SR' and 'turn' are also used generically for any short-row method (German, Japanese, shadow). W&T is one specific short-row technique.

rs: On a knit row: Slip next stitch purlwise to right needle
Bring yarn to front (between needles)
Slip stitch back to left needle
Turn work — you are now on the WS
Later: when working past the wrap, pick up the wrap with the stitch and work together
ws: On a purl row: Slip next stitch purlwise to right needle
Slip stitch back to left needle
Turn work — you are now on the RS

semantic_description: A left-facing chart-direction variant of wrap-and-turn short-row shaping. The underlying knitting technique is the standard wrap-and-turn method, but the symbol specifically indicates the turning point oriented toward the left side of the chart or fabric.

written_forms: wrap and turn left
left-facing short-row wrap and turn
ambiguity_notes: Directional short row. The 'left' qualifier indicates the direction of the turn/shaping. Some pattern systems use 'w&t RS' and 'w&t WS' rather than left/right.

rs: Work a standard wrap and turn at this point
Interpret the symbol as the left-facing chart-direction form of wrap and turn

semantic_description: A right-facing chart-direction variant of wrap-and-turn short-row shaping. The underlying knitting technique is the standard wrap-and-turn method, but the symbol specifically indicates the turning point oriented toward the right side of the chart or fabric.

written_forms: wrap and turn right
right-facing short-row wrap and turn

rs: Work a standard wrap and turn at this point
Interpret the symbol as the right-facing chart-direction form of wrap and turn

semantic_description: An alternative short-row technique that uses a 'double stitch' instead of a wrap. After turning, the first stitch is pulled up tight so both legs are visible on the needle (creating a double stitch). When later worked, the double stitch is knit or purled through both legs. Produces a neater result than wrap-and-turn with less visible holes. Increasingly popular as the standard short-row method.

written_forms: turn work, slip first stitch purlwise with yarn in front, pull yarn firmly over needle to create a double stitch
German short row: after turning, pull first stitch tight so both legs show
ambiguity_notes: 'DS' (double stitch) is the most common abbreviation for the German short row technique, named for the characteristic double loop created when turning. 'DM' comes from the German 'Doppelmasche'. 'GSR' is used by some English-language designers. 'DS' can collide with 'drop stitch' — context is key.
'DS' = 'double stitch' in German short-row context; don't confuse with 'drop stitch'.

rs: Turn work at the desired point
With yarn in front, slip the first stitch purlwise
Pull the working yarn firmly over the needle toward the back — the stitch legs will pull up, making a 'double stitch' that looks like two stitches
Continue working the row
When you reach the double stitch on a later row, work both legs together as one stitch

semantic_description: Knits into the stitch one row below the next stitch on the needle, rather than the stitch itself. The current stitch drops off and unravels one row, while the new stitch is formed from the row below. Creates a textured, elongated stitch. Used in fisherman's rib (half-brioche), waffle stitch, and some textured patterns. A simpler alternative to brioche knitting that produces a similar double-fabric effect.

written_forms: insert needle into the stitch directly below the next stitch on the left needle, knit it, and let the upper stitch drop
knit into the center of the stitch one row below
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'k1B' (knit stitch in row below). In chart form this appears as a downward-pointing arrow or a special symbol. 'k1b' is extremely common. CRITICAL COLLISION: 'k1b' / 'K1B' is also used in brioche knitting by some designers to mean 'brioche knit' (as a substitute for 'brk') — particularly in older patterns pre-Marchant standardization, and by designers who don't use the brk/brp system. Always check legend. When 'k1b' appears in a brioche pattern context, it maps to brk.
'k1b' can be confused with 'k1tbl' (knit 1 through back loop). Context matters — 'b' can mean 'below' or 'back' depending on the pattern.

rs: Insert right needle into the center of the stitch directly below the next stitch on the left needle (one row down)
Wrap yarn and knit through this lower stitch
Let the upper stitch (on the left needle) drop off — it unravels and is absorbed

semantic_description: Purls into the stitch one row below the next stitch on the needle. The purl-side equivalent of k1-below. Used in fisherman's rib and textured patterns.

written_forms: insert needle into the stitch one row below purlwise, purl it, drop the upper stitch
ambiguity_notes: Same caveat as k1b: in brioche patterns, 'p1b' can mean brioche purl (brp). In non-brioche patterns, it means purl into the stitch in the row below (elongated purl). Verify with legend.

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle into the stitch one row below the next stitch purlwise
Purl through the lower stitch
Drop the upper stitch off left needle

semantic_description: A twisted dip-stitch variant of knit 1 below. The new stitch is formed from the stitch in the row below but worked in a twisted orientation, producing a tighter and more defined elongated stitch than an ordinary k1-below. Japanese chart systems and Marnen's JIS-derived symbol set treat twisted dip stitch as a distinct operation from a plain dip stitch.

written_forms: insert the needle into the stitch below and knit it in a twisted orientation
work the stitch below through the back loop or crossed orientation, then let the upper stitch drop
ambiguity_notes: A relatively rare texture stitch — knit into the row below and twist the resulting stitch. Used in some Scandinavian/Nordic stitch dictionaries. 'k1btbl' is descriptive. No CYC entry.
This is distinct from ordinary k1-below. The symbol means the dipped stitch is twisted, not merely elongated.

Some pattern glossaries describe the exact needle path as 'through the back loop' while others describe it as 'crossed' or 'twisted' — the key distinction is the twisted orientation of the dipped stitch.

rs: Insert the right needle into the stitch one row below the next stitch on the left needle
Work that lower stitch in the twisted or back-loop orientation specified by the pattern
Allow the upper stitch to drop off the left needle

semantic_description: Intentionally drops a stitch off the needle and allows it to ladder down (unravel) one or more rows. Creates an elongated, open ladder effect in the fabric. Usually preceded by a yarn-over or double wrap on a previous row so the ladder has a defined starting point. Used in drop-stitch patterns for dramatic open textures.

written_forms: drop the next stitch off the needle and allow it to ladder down
drop stitch: let the next stitch fall off the needle
drop the next stitch off the needle and let it ladder down
ambiguity_notes: 'DS' collides with German short row ('double stitch'). In drop-stitch lace and intentional ladder patterns, 'drop st' is unambiguous. In a chart cell, the symbol is often a cross or empty cell with a dropped bar. 'drop' alone should not collide in a chart legend context.
DS also collides with German short row (ds/DM). Disambiguate by context.
'DS' can also mean 'double stitch' (German short row). Legend-dependent.

rs: Slip the next stitch off the left needle without working it
Allow it to unravel/ladder down to the desired point

semantic_description: Places a pre-strung bead at a specific stitch position. Beads are strung on the yarn before knitting begins. At the designated position, a bead is slid up to sit in front of (or around) the stitch. Multiple beading techniques exist: slip-stitch beading (most common), hook beading, and carry-along beading. The stitch count does not change.

written_forms: slide bead up to sit in front of the next stitch, then slip the stitch purlwise
place bead: using crochet hook, place bead over the stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'B' is deeply ambiguous: bobble, bead, or merely a letter label. In beaded knitting, 'PB' (place bead) is cleaner. 'SB' (slide bead) is used when working with pre-strung beads. Always verify with legend. If both beads and bobbles appear in the same pattern, avoid bare 'B' as an abbreviation for either.
'B' and 'PB' can also mean bobble or purl-through-back-loop in other pattern conventions. Disambiguate via the legend.

rs: Slide a pre-strung bead up the working yarn to sit next to the needles
Slip the next stitch purlwise with yarn in front
The bead sits in front of the slipped stitch
tools: optional: small crochet hook for hook method

semantic_description: Places a bead and works the associated stitch as a knit stitch. This is more specific than a generic bead symbol and should be kept separate from bead purl in dictionaries and lookup systems that care about the worked stitch type.

written_forms: slide the bead into place and knit the next stitch
place bead, knit
ambiguity_notes: No CYC standard. In beaded knitting, the method of placing the bead (hook method, slip method) varies by technique. 'BK' is a reasonable abbreviation not in common publisher use.

rs: Slide a pre-strung bead up the working yarn to the stitch position
Knit the beaded stitch as specified by the pattern
tools: optional: small crochet hook for hook method

semantic_description: Places a bead and works the associated stitch as a purl stitch. This is distinct from bead knit when a chart legend or symbol library encodes the stitch type explicitly.

written_forms: slide the bead into place and purl the next stitch
place bead, purl
ambiguity_notes: Same situation as bead_knit. 'BP' is reasonable. Pattern-defined.

rs: Slide a pre-strung bead up the working yarn to the stitch position
Purl the beaded stitch as specified by the pattern
tools: optional: small crochet hook for hook method

semantic_description: Colorwork technique to trap a long carried strand (float) by catching it behind a working stitch. The working stitch is still completed normally; only the float is trapped. Used in stranded colorwork to prevent long floats from snagging.

written_forms: catch the floating strand behind the working stitch
ambiguity_notes: In stranded colorwork, catching/trapping floats is a technique instruction, not a stitch operation per se. It rarely appears as a chart symbol but may appear in legends of long-float colorwork patterns. 'CF' for catch float is reasonable but uncommon.

rs: Bring the carried strand under the working yarn before completing the next stitch, trapping the float against the back of the work

semantic_description: A knit stitch worked in the Scandinavian twined knitting tradition (tvåändsstickning), using two strands of yarn alternately and twisting them around each other between stitches. Produces a denser, more structured fabric than regular knitting.

written_forms: work a knit stitch using the back strand, alternating with the front strand
ambiguity_notes: Tvåändsstickning (Swedish twined knitting) uses two strands alternated. 'tvk' comes from Swedish 'tvåändsstickning knit'. Not in CYC. Only appears in twined knitting pattern contexts (Swedish, Nordic). Very niche.

rs: Knit the stitch using the strand currently held behind the other working strand
Twist the two strands around each other

semantic_description: A purl stitch worked in the Scandinavian twined knitting tradition. WS equivalent of twined_knit.

written_forms: work a purl stitch using the back strand, alternating with the front strand
ambiguity_notes: Purl equivalent of twined knit. Same niche context.

rs: Purl the stitch using the strand currently held behind
Twist the two strands around each other

semantic_description: A decorative round in twined knitting where both yarns cross systematically to form a surface chain or braid effect. Related to Latvian braids; common in traditional Scandinavian colorwork. Produces a raised horizontal decorative band.

written_forms: work both strands across, crossing them between every stitch to form a braid
ambiguity_notes: No standard English abbreviation. The Latvian braid and twined braid are worked by alternating yarn strands in a specific pattern. Pattern-defined in all sources. 'TV braid' references tvåändsstickning.

rs: Bring both yarns to the front
Work each stitch alternately, crossing the yarns between stitches in the same direction across the round

semantic_description: A twined (tvåändstickning) knit stitch worked through the back loop, using two strands of yarn alternated between stitches with the carrying strand twisted in a consistent direction between each stitch. Produces a dense, weatherproof fabric typical of Scandinavian tvåändstickning (two-end knitting).

written_forms: knit 1 through back loop using twined method
ambiguity_notes: Not in CYC. Used primarily in Scandinavian / Swedish twined knitting patterns.

rs: Alternate the two strands of yarn, knitting 1 through the back loop with the current active strand
Twist the two strands consistently between each stitch

semantic_description: A twined (tvåändstickning) purl stitch worked through the back loop, using two strands of yarn. The mirror of twined_knit_tbl for WS-facing or textured patterning within twined knitting.

written_forms: purl 1 through back loop using twined method
ambiguity_notes: Not in CYC. Scandinavian twined knitting only.

rs: Alternate the two strands; purl 1 through the back loop with the active strand
Twist the strands between each stitch
